refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement; it depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational conditions beyond the FR scope. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; meeting the target is a design and operational outcome.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved) over the agreed evaluation period, computes energy_per_ton, and reports it in a format accessible to the Site Manager and Project Manager for comparison against E_target. Acceptance: reported energy_per_ton is traceable, auditable, and matches independent measurement within ±5%.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total energy consumed (electrical, fuel, or equivalent) during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total work output (tons moved) during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy_per_ton as the ratio of total energy consumed to total work output.

FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy_per_ton and supporting metrics (total energy, total output, evaluation period) to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Project Manager) in a human-readable and machine-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as the ratio of aggregated energy over aggregated tonnage at configurable reporting intervals.

DP2: Embed energy metering at the prime mover (engine/motor) output shaft; embed load/displacement sensors on the work mechanism; derive work output in energy-equivalent units; compute energy_per_ton by normalizing prime mover energy consumption by work-equivalent output.

DP3: Establish a baseline energy consumption model (calibrated under controlled conditions); measure actual energy and tonnage in real-time; compute energy_per_ton as actual energy divided by actual tonnage; flag deviations from baseline as efficiency loss indicators.

DP4: Instrument energy input at the source; instrument work output via independent tonnage counter; synchronize measurements via a common time reference; aggregate over the evaluation period; compute energy_per_ton and compare against E_target; log all raw and derived data for audit and traceability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Instrument energy input at the source; instrument work output via independent tonnage counter; synchronize measurements via a common time reference; aggregate over the evaluation period; compute energy_per_ton and compare against E_target; log all raw and derived data for audit and traceability.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and tonnage measurement are logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor domains), reducing coupling risk.

Time synchronization is a shared dependency; if clocks drift, both energy and tonnage aggregation windows may misalign. Mitigation: use a single authoritative time source (e.g., GPS, NTP, or local RTC with periodic validation).
Data aggregation logic (summation, ratio computation) is centralized, reducing duplication and coupling across reporting modules.
Comparison against E_target is a simple threshold check; no feedback loop to energy or tonnage measurement, avoiding control-loop coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves low coupling by separating measurement concerns (energy vs. tonnage) and deferring aggregation and comparison to a single, stateless computation. DP1 and DP2 introduce tighter coupling between energy and work domains (DP2 requires energy-equivalent normalization, increasing complexity). DP3 introduces model-based coupling, which increases risk if baseline assumptions change.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is measurable at a single point (e.g., fuel flow, electrical power input). RISK: If energy is distributed (e.g., multiple fuel tanks, hybrid power), measurement may require aggregation across multiple sources; design must account for this.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage moved is directly observable and countable (e.g., via load cells, displacement sensors, or material counters). RISK: If tonnage is inferred indirectly (e.g., from bucket counts or cycle times), measurement error may propagate; validation against ground truth is required.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is well-defined and operationally enforced (e.g., shift, day, week). RISK: If period boundaries are ambiguous or overlapping, aggregation windows may be inconsistent; clear operational procedures are required.

ASSUMPTION: Time synchronization between energy and tonnage measurement systems is achievable and maintainable. RISK: If systems use independent clocks, drift may cause misalignment; periodic validation and resynchronization are required.

ASSUMPTION: E_target is fixed and known at design time. RISK: If E_target is customer-variable or subject to renegotiation, the comparison logic must be parameterized; hard-coded thresholds will require rework.

ASSUMPTION: Data logging and audit trail are required for compliance or dispute resolution. RISK: If logging is incomplete or tampered with, energy_per_ton claims may be unverifiable; secure, immutable logging is recommended.

ASSUMPTION: Measurement accuracy and resolution are sufficient to detect meaningful deviations from E_target. RISK: If sensor noise or quantization error exceeds acceptable tolerance, reported energy_per_ton may be unreliable; sensor selection and calibration are critical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR shall be refined to explicitly state: (1) the required measurement accuracy and resolution for energy and tonnage; (2) the definition of the evaluation period and how period boundaries are determined operationally; (3) whether E_target is fixed or customer-parameterized; (4) the required data retention and audit trail policy; (5) the acceptable time synchronization tolerance between energy and tonnage measurement systems. These refinements will constrain DP selection and IM packaging, reducing ambiguity in implementation and validation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy monitoring, measurement instrumentation, data aggregation, performance computation, and audit logging for the autonomous hauling system

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit raw energy input measurements, tonnage counts, synchronized timestamps, computed energy_per_ton, and comparison results against E_target to external monitoring systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive evaluation period parameters, E_target thresholds, and instrumentation configuration directives

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts if energy_per_ton exceeds E_target or if measurement integrity is compromised

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronize time reference with external time source; transmit audit logs and raw/derived data for traceability

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Energy instrumentation, tonnage measurement, time synchronization, aggregation, computation, and audit logging are all core to the NGHE (Next-Generation Hauling Efficiency) solution mandate. The DP is fundamentally a performance monitoring and optimization subsystem. Minor diffusion: Vehicle must provide raw energy and tonnage signals; Teleoperation may request real-time performance dashboards; Safety Services may consume alerts.

Poor containment. Vehicle is responsible for propulsion, steering, and mechanical operation. While it generates energy and tonnage signals, it should not own the measurement instrumentation, synchronization logic, aggregation, or audit logging. These are system-level concerns that transcend a single vehicle instance. High diffusion: computation and logging would leak into fleet management and monitoring layers.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command channel. Energy measurement, aggregation, and performance computation are not teleoperation concerns. Placing DP here would scatter measurement logic across the control interface, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: would require duplication of computation logic in multiple teleoperation instances.

Poor containment. Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection, emergency response, and safety constraints. While it may consume energy_per_ton alerts to enforce safety limits, it should not own the measurement instrumentation, aggregation, or audit logging. These are performance monitoring concerns, not safety-critical control. High diffusion: measurement logic would be entangled with safety logic, complicating both.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of DP4 because energy efficiency monitoring and performance optimization are the core mission of the Next-Generation Hauling Efficiency system. All measurement, aggregation, computation, and audit functions are cohesive within this boundary. External modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) interact with NGHE Solution via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry for data export, Tasking for configuration, SafetyAlert for threshold violations, Network Link for time sync and log transmission). This design preserves modularity and prevents measurement logic from diffusing into vehicle control, human interfaces, or safety systems.
containment_risks: Vehicle must reliably provide raw energy input and tonnage signals with sufficient fidelity; if Vehicle instrumentation is unreliable, NGHE Solution cannot compute valid energy_per_ton.
Time synchronization via Network Link must be robust; clock drift or network latency could invalidate synchronized measurements across distributed energy and tonnage sensors.
Audit logging volume may grow rapidly; NGHE Solution must manage storage and transmission bandwidth without impacting real-time performance computation.
Safety Services must be able to consume energy_per_ton alerts with low latency; if NGHE Solution computation is delayed, safety response may be sluggish.
If multiple Vehicle instances report energy and tonnage concurrently, NGHE Solution must handle aggregation and synchronization correctly to avoid double-counting or race conditions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly and completely addresses FR by specifying all necessary steps: instrument energy input, instrument work output, synchronize via common time reference, aggregate over evaluation period, compute energy_per_ton, compare against E_target, and log for audit. The DP is solution-agnostic and logically sound. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from DP4 outputs.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle module (diffusion_score 0.65): Raw energy and tonnage signal generation is appropriately retained in Vehicle, but Vehicle must not own aggregation, synchronization, or computation logic. Risk: if Vehicle attempts to pre-process or filter signals before transmission to NGHE Solution, measurement integrity and auditability degrade.

Teleoperation module (diffusion_score 0.75): High risk of logic scatter if real-time performance dashboards or manual threshold adjustments are implemented in Teleoperation. Risk: computation logic duplicated across teleoperation instances; inconsistent energy_per_ton calculations.

Safety Services module (diffusion_score 0.6): Alert consumption is appropriate, but Safety Services must not own measurement or aggregation. Risk: if Safety Services attempts to compute energy_per_ton independently to enforce safety limits, measurement logic becomes entangled and audit trail is compromised.

Time synchronization via Network Link: Clock drift or network latency could invalidate synchronized measurements. Risk: if time reference is not reliably maintained, energy and tonnage measurements may be misaligned, producing incorrect energy_per_ton.

Concurrent multi-vehicle aggregation: If multiple Vehicle instances report energy and tonnage concurrently, race conditions or double-counting could occur during aggregation. Risk: energy_per_ton computed over fleet may be incorrect if synchronization and aggregation logic is not atomic or properly ordered.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves excellent containment (0.92) as the natural owner of DP4. All measurement instrumentation, synchronization, aggregation, computation, and audit logging are cohesive within this boundary and aligned with the Next-Generation Hauling Efficiency mission. External modules interact via well-defined interfaces: Vehicle provides raw signals (Telemetry in), Tasking configures evaluation parameters and E_target, SafetyAlert exports threshold violations, Network Link synchronizes time and transmits audit logs. This design preserves separation of concerns and prevents measurement logic from diffusing into vehicle control, human interfaces, or safety systems. Minor diffusion is acceptable and managed via interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly verifiable from NGHE Solution outputs. Raw energy input, tonnage counts, synchronized timestamps, computed energy_per_ton, and comparison results are all logged for audit and traceability. Verification requires: (1) validation that Vehicle provides energy and tonnage signals with sufficient fidelity and accuracy; (2) confirmation that time synchronization via Network Link maintains clock alignment within acceptable bounds; (3) inspection of audit logs to confirm aggregation and computation are correct over the evaluation period. Worksite safety plan and operational policies must define acceptable measurement latency and alert response time.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns measurement instrumentation, synchronization, aggregation, computation, and audit logging. Vehicle owns energy and tonnage signal generation. Teleoperation owns human-machine interface and command dispatch (does not own computation). Safety Services owns hazard detection and emergency response (consumes alerts, does not own measurement). Tasking owns evaluation period parameters and E_target configuration. Network Link owns time synchronization and log transmission. All interfaces are explicitly defined with direction and details. Ownership boundaries are non-overlapping and responsibilities are cohesive within each module.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly specify time synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±100 ms) in Network Link interface contract to ensure energy and tonnage measurements remain synchronized within acceptable bounds.
Define audit logging schema and retention policy in NGHE Solution to manage storage and transmission bandwidth without impacting real-time performance computation.

Specify Vehicle signal fidelity requirements (e.g., energy measurement accuracy ±5%, tonnage counter resolution ±0.1 ton) in Telemetry interface contract to ensure NGHE Solution can compute valid energy_per_ton.
Define alert latency requirement (e.g., SafetyAlert must be emitted within 5 seconds of energy_per_ton exceeding E_target) to ensure Safety Services can respond in time.
Specify aggregation and synchronization logic for concurrent multi-vehicle reporting (e.g., atomic transactions, ordered message processing) to prevent race conditions and double-counting.

Add acceptance criterion for audit log completeness: all raw energy input, tonnage counts, synchronized timestamps, and computed energy_per_ton values must be logged and retrievable for the entire evaluation period.

decision.justification: FR4 and DP4 pass both FR-DP and DP-IM gates with strong logical coherence and excellent module containment (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP4's correctness depends critically on several sub-system behaviors that are not yet decomposed: (1) time synchronization tolerance and clock drift management, (2) Vehicle signal fidelity and measurement accuracy, (3) concurrent multi-vehicle aggregation atomicity and ordering, (4) audit logging schema and retention, and (5) alert latency guarantees. These are not implementation details—they are functional requirements that must be explicitly derived, designed, and verified to ensure DP4 can actually achieve FR4 in a safety-critical, multi-vehicle, real-time environment. Stopping here would leave critical dependencies unspecified and verification infeasible. Decomposing these sub-FRs will expose interface contracts, timing constraints, and failure modes that must be managed at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Maintain synchronized time reference across Vehicle, NGHE Solution, and Safety Services modules such that energy and tonnage measurements remain temporally aligned within ±100 ms tolerance for the duration of the evaluation period.

FR4.2: Acquire and validate energy input signals from Vehicle with measurement accuracy of ±5% and sufficient temporal resolution to compute instantaneous power and cumulative energy over the evaluation period.
FR4.3: Acquire and validate tonnage output signals from Vehicle with counter resolution of ±0.1 ton and sufficient temporal resolution to compute cumulative tonnage over the evaluation period.
FR4.4: Aggregate energy and tonnage measurements from concurrent multi-vehicle reporting without race conditions, double-counting, or loss of data, ensuring atomic and ordered processing of signals.

FR4.5: Log all raw energy input, tonnage counts, synchronized timestamps, and computed energy_per_ton values to persistent audit storage with complete traceability and retrievability for the entire evaluation period.
FR4.6: Emit SafetyAlert to Safety Services module within 5 seconds of energy_per_ton exceeding E_target, with sufficient latency margin to enable timely hazard response and operator intervention.

revise.why: No revisions to FR4, DP4, or IM allocation are required at this level. All gates pass and recommended_revisions are implementation-level interface contracts that will be derived and detailed during sub-FR decomposition. The current level is sound; decomposition is the appropriate next step.

assumptions: Vehicle provides raw energy and tonnage signals with sufficient fidelity and temporal resolution to support energy_per_ton computation.
Network Link can maintain time synchronization across all modules within acceptable tolerance (±100 ms assumed; to be validated).
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to aggregate, synchronize, and log signals from multiple concurrent vehicles without performance degradation.
Safety Services can respond to SafetyAlert within operational latency requirements (5 seconds assumed; to be validated against worksite safety plan).
Audit logging does not introduce unacceptable latency or bandwidth overhead to real-time energy_per_ton computation.
Concurrent multi-vehicle reporting can be serialized or made atomic without violating real-time performance constraints.
open_risks: Time synchronization drift: Clock drift or network latency could invalidate synchronized measurements if tolerance is not maintained. Mitigation: derive FR4.1 with explicit tolerance specification and clock synchronization protocol.

Vehicle signal fidelity: If Vehicle provides inaccurate or low-resolution energy/tonnage signals, energy_per_ton computation will be invalid. Mitigation: derive FR4.2 and FR4.3 with explicit accuracy and resolution requirements; validate against Vehicle design.

Concurrent aggregation race conditions: Multiple vehicles reporting simultaneously could cause double-counting or data loss if aggregation logic is not atomic. Mitigation: derive FR4.4 with explicit ordering and atomicity constraints; implement transactional or message-queue-based aggregation.

Audit log scalability: High-frequency logging from multiple vehicles could exceed storage or transmission bandwidth. Mitigation: derive FR4.5 with explicit schema, retention policy, and compression strategy.

Alert latency: If SafetyAlert is delayed beyond operator response time, safety effectiveness is compromised. Mitigation: derive FR4.6 with explicit latency requirement and real-time scheduling constraints.

Safety criticality: High safety criticality of energy efficiency monitoring (potential for operator override or hazardous behavior if alerts are delayed or unreliable) requires rigorous verification of all sub-FRs and interface contracts.
evidence_needed: Vehicle design specification: energy measurement accuracy, tonnage counter resolution, signal transmission frequency, and signal format.
Network Link specification: time synchronization protocol, clock drift tolerance, network latency bounds, and message ordering guarantees.
NGHE Solution performance specification: computational capacity, storage capacity, real-time scheduling constraints, and audit logging throughput.
Safety Services specification: alert consumption latency, hazard response procedures, and operator intervention time requirements.
Worksite safety plan: acceptable measurement latency, alert response time, and operational policies for energy efficiency monitoring.
Verification plan: test cases for time synchronization, signal fidelity, concurrent aggregation, audit log completeness, and alert latency under nominal and fault conditions.
